AI/ML learning path

Prompt engineering and RAG

Systematic prompt design, retrieval-augmented generation pipelines, and techniques for grounding LLM outputs in verified knowledge.

Module progress 0%

0 of 3 lessons complete

Next up Lesson 1: Prompt engineering

Zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, and structured prompting techniques for reliable LLM outputs.

Why this module matters Build one clean mental model before scaling it.

Move through the lessons in order so each trade-off has context before the harder distributed systems decisions show up.

Study target 3 lessons left

Aim to finish one lesson, then explain its core trade-off out loud before moving on.

AI Engineer learning path 3 lessons 0/3 complete Follow in order for best results Back to AI/ML roadmap Start module View lesson runway

Learning rhythm

Study this like a sequenced course, not a reference dump

  1. Preview the runway. Skim every lesson title first so you can see the progression before you dive in.
  2. Do one lesson deeply. Read the summary, inspect the diagram, and identify the sharpest trade-off.
  3. Say it back aloud. Explain the idea in interviewer language before you mark it complete.
  4. Only then advance. Move to the next lesson when you can defend the previous one without the notes open.
AI/ML sequence Designed for interview recall

Outcomes

What you should walk away with

  • Design effective prompts using few-shot, chain-of-thought, and structured techniques
  • Build end-to-end RAG pipelines that ground LLM responses in external knowledge
  • Evaluate and optimize retrieval quality and generation accuracy

Practice flow

How to study each lesson

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Mastery check

How you know it is sticking

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson runway

Follow the sequence from first principles to fluency

This is the core pattern borrowed from stronger learning products: show the path, make the next step obvious, and keep outcomes visible while the learner moves.

25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 1

Prompt engineering

Open

Zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, and structured prompting techniques for reliable LLM outputs.

Why it matters

Effective prompting is the fastest way to extract value from LLMs without training. It is the primary interface skill for AI engineers building applications.

What you will cover

Core techniquesStructured outputEvaluation and iteration

Good interview signal

  • Can select the appropriate prompting technique for a given task complexity.
  • Designs prompts that produce structured, parseable outputs.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 2

Retrieval-augmented generation

Open

End-to-end RAG architecture: document ingestion, chunking, retrieval, context assembly, and grounded generation.

Why it matters

RAG is the dominant pattern for building knowledge-grounded AI applications that need up-to-date information without retraining the model.

What you will cover

RAG architectureAdvanced RAG patternsEvaluation and optimization

Good interview signal

  • Can design a complete RAG pipeline from document ingestion to response generation.
  • Understands chunking tradeoffs and their impact on retrieval quality.
35-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson

Lesson 3

Building with LLM frameworks

Open

LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and other orchestration frameworks for composing LLM-powered applications.

Why it matters

Frameworks abstract common patterns (chains, retrieval, memory) and accelerate development of production LLM applications.

What you will cover

Framework landscapeCore abstractionsWhen to use vs build custom

Good interview signal

  • Can choose an appropriate framework for the project requirements.
  • Understands framework abstractions well enough to debug and extend them.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections
Start lesson
